step_1_context.text: When we talk about two events or activities that went on over the same period of past time, we can often use the past continuous or the past simple for both:

step_2_explanation.simple_rule:
When we talk about two events or activities that went on over the same period of past time, we can often use the past continuous or the past simple for both:
 • Mia was reading to the children while Ben was washing up.
step_2_explanation.formula:
We usually use the past simple rather than the past continuous to talk about repeated past actions:
 • We went to Spain three times last year.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake: We often use the past simple in a narrative (e.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 4.1.1 Just as I ___ into the bath, all the lights ___ off. (get / go)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Past continuous for the situation in progress, past simple for the interrupting event.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 4.1.2 I ___ to go away this weekend, but my boss ___ me some work that I have to finish by Monday. (hope / give)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Unfulfilled intention vs the single completed event that prevented it.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 4.1.3 When I ___ in Paris, I ___ three hours a day travelling to and from work. (live / spend)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Background situation vs repeated past action.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 4.1.4 A friendly American couple ___ to him as he ___ at the hotel reception. (start / check in)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Single event interrupting a background situation.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 4.1.5 I bumped into Lena last week. She ___ a lot better than when I last ___ her. (look / see)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Temporary past situation vs single event.
